Headline: The World Wide Web
Text: From an open dream at CERN to a handful of platforms — and a fight over who governs it. This story follows where the Web spread, and the people and places the official version skips.

Headline: The Open Dream: A Proposal at CERN
Text: In March 1989 Tim Berners-Lee proposed a hypertext system for CERN; by Christmas 1990 the first website was running on his NeXT computer, and on 30 April 1993 CERN placed the Web's software in the public domain — free for everyone, forever.
Media Caption: The NeXT computer Tim Berners-Lee used at CERN — the world's first web server.
Alt Text: A black NeXT cube computer with a handwritten sticker warning not to power it down
Source: CERN — The birth of the Web

Headline: Mosaic Makes the Web Visual
Text: Released at the University of Illinois' NCSA in 1993, Mosaic put images beside text behind a friendly point-and-click interface — the browser that carried the Web out of the physics labs and into living rooms.
Media Caption: NCSA Mosaic, the browser that put images on the Web.
Alt Text: Screenshot of the NCSA Mosaic browser window showing an early web page with inline images

Headline: Who Governs the Standards? W3C Founded
Text: Tim Berners-Lee left CERN to found the World Wide Web Consortium at MIT, an international body to keep the Web's core standards open — the first institutional answer to who governs the Web.
Media Caption: The W3C masthead as served by w3.org in June 1997.
Alt Text: The 1997 W3C logo: a large W3C wordmark beside the words World Wide Web Consortium

Headline: The Web Learns to Sell: The First Banner Ad
Text: HotWired ran the Web's first banner ad — AT&T's "You Will" campaign; almost half of everyone who saw it clicked. Advertising became the business model that would fund, and reshape, the "free" Web.
Source: Wired — Oct. 27, 1994: Web Gives Birth to Banner Ads

Headline: The Netscape IPO Starts the Gold Rush
Text: Shares of Netscape, a browser company that had never turned a profit, doubled on their first day of trading — the moment the open Web became a market and the dot-com boom began.
Media Caption: Netscape Navigator 2, the browser that carried the company to its IPO.
Alt Text: Screenshot of the Netscape Navigator 2 browser window

Headline: The Browser Wars
Text: Microsoft bundled Internet Explorer into Windows to crush Netscape; the fight ended in a courtroom — United States v. Microsoft, the first great legal battle over who controls the Web's front door.
Media Caption: Usage share during the first browser war: Netscape falls as Internet Explorer rises.
Alt Text: Line chart of browser usage share from 1994 to 2009, showing Netscape declining as Internet Explorer peaks
Source: U.S. Department of Justice — United States v. Microsoft

Headline: The Weird Creative Web: Flash
Text: Macromedia's Flash turned the browser into a canvas for games, cartoons and net art. Adobe switched it off on 31 December 2020 and a whole creative layer of the Web went dark — saved only by volunteer archivists.
Media Caption: "Badger Badger Badger" (2003), one of the Flash loops preserved by the Internet Archive's emulator.
Alt Text: Cartoon badgers dancing in a green field — a frame from the Flash animation Badger Badger Badger
Source: Internet Archive — Flash Animations Live Forever

Headline: Search Becomes the Front Door
Text: Google incorporated in a Menlo Park garage; as search became how most people find the Web, one company's ranking algorithm quietly became the index of everything.
Media Caption: The garage at 232 Santa Margarita Avenue, Menlo Park, where Google incorporated in 1998.
Alt Text: A suburban garage with a basketball hoop above the driveway

Headline: Everyone a Publisher: Blogs
Text: Blogger, launched in San Francisco in 1999, made publishing a button click. For a moment the Web's promise was millions of independent voices — most of them later folded back into platform feeds.

Headline: The Platform Era Begins
Text: 'Thefacebook' opened to Harvard students in February 2004; within two decades a handful of platforms, not the open web of pages, would hold most attention, advertising and public debate.
Alt Text: Screenshot of the minimal Thefacebook welcome page from 2004

Headline: The Invisible Cleaners of the Web
Text: Every feed is kept 'clean' by people: tens of thousands of commercial content moderators, many of them in Manila, view and delete the Web's most disturbing material so that users never see it — labour the platform story skips.
Source: Sarah T. Roberts, Behind the Screen (Yale University Press, 2019)

Headline: The Fight Over Governance: GDPR
Text: Adopted in 2016 and enforced from May 2018, the EU's General Data Protection Regulation was the first sweeping attempt to regulate what platforms do with personal data — the fight over the Web's rules moved from standards bodies to parliaments. The map draws the Union as GDPR found it — the United Kingdom, mid-Brexit, still inside.
